behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix
behaviors.justification: The behavior depends on discovering .sql files from a real filesystem and ordering them. A Unit test could verify the sorting algorithm in isolation, but would miss real filesystem failures: directory traversal edge cases, special character handling, symlinks, or OS-specific ordering behavior. Integration with a real (temporary) filesystem catches these.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary directory with migration files named with numeric prefixes (001-users.sql, 002-posts.sql, 010-comments.sql, etc.). Invoke the discovery and ordering logic against this real filesystem. Verify that the returned migration list is in strictly ascending numeric order, regardless of filesystem listing order.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run
behaviors.justification: The behavior requires verifying that the runner correctly checks the _migrations table before re-running a migration. A Unit test with a mocked database could verify the check logic, but would miss real failures: transaction isolation issues, race conditions during the check, or bugs in metadata table queries. Integration with a real database ensures the idempotency check works correctly in the actual environment.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a real PostgreSQL database with the _migrations table. Run migration #1 successfully. Query the database to confirm the entry in _migrations. Run the migration runner again against the same database. Verify that migration #1 is not re-executed (by checking migration file hasn't been applied again) and the database state is unchanged since the first run.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations are attempted
behaviors.justification: The behavior depends on real database transaction semantics—that changes are actually rolled back when a statement fails and the runner halts. A Unit test with a mocked transaction could pass even if the code doesn't use transactions at all. Integration with a real database and a real failing SQL statement catches actual rollback failures, incomplete rollback, or missing error handling that would leave the database in an inconsistent state.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a real PostgreSQL database. Create two migration files: (1) a valid migration that succeeds, and (2) a migration with intentionally invalid SQL that will fail. Run the migration runner. Verify: (a) the first migration is applied and recorded, (b) the second migration fails with an error, (c) any partial changes from the second migration are rolled back (query the database to confirm), and (d) no attempt is made to run any third migration.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start simultaneously, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances
behaviors.justification: The behavior requires testing real concurrent access to the database from separate process instances. A Unit or even Integration test in a single process (with async/threading) cannot catch real concurrency failures: race conditions between checking and writing to _migrations, database lock contention, lost updates, or deadlocks. System tests with two actual Node.js processes running simultaneously against the same database verify that OS-level concurrency and database locking prevent duplicate application.
behaviors.test_description: Spawn two separate Node.js child processes, each running the migration runner against the same real PostgreSQL database. Coordinate them to start at approximately the same time using a synchronization mechanism (e.g., shared lock file or database signal). Let both run to completion. Query the _migrations table and verify: (a) each migration has exactly one entry (no duplicates), (b) both processes complete without error, (c) the database is in a consistent state, and (d) all expected migrations are recorded.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied
behaviors.justification: The behavior requires verifying that the _migrations table is created before migrations run, with the correct schema. A Unit test with a mocked table creation would miss real failures: schema mismatches, permission errors during table creation, or bugs that cause migrations to run before the table exists. Integration with a real database verifies the actual table creation and ensures the runner's initialization is correct.
behaviors.test_description: Create a fresh PostgreSQL database (or use a temporary database) that does not have a _migrations table. Place migration files in the migrations directory. Run the migration runner. Verify that: (a) the _migrations table exists after the run, (b) it has the correct schema (columns for migration name, applied timestamp, etc.), (c) all applied migrations are recorded in the table, and (d) no migration file was applied before the table existed (by checking the table's first entry timestamp or by verifying the database state matches expectations).
